PEER REVIEW BY SHARED SERVICES
CANADA OF THE ADVANCED
ANALYTICS TRIAGE FOR VISITOR
RECORD APPLICATIONS
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
1
Executive Summary
The peer review report assesses an Artificial Intelligence application developed by
Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) for the Visitor Record (VR) program.
An assessment is a mandatory requirement of the Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat’s
(TBS) Directive on Automated Decision-Making and evaluates the IRCC’s machine learning
models that assist with the eligibility assessment for all visitor record applications. The
IRCC application under review flags uncomplicated applications for automated eligibility
approval by leveraging a series of rules developed from IRCC personnel in tandem with a
custom machine learning model. The assessment finds that IRCC’s solution is robust, and
the necessary controls and safeguards have been sufficiently described to ensure
applicant privacy is preserved throughout its lifecycle.
Approach
During the peer review process, a team of Shared Services Canada (SSC) data scientists
and business analysts worked with IRCC to understand the Visitor Request process and
the AI support tools. The SSC team referred to the Bronson/Millar Peer Review for
Automated Decision-Making Tools Under Canada’s Directive on Automated Decision-
Making – A Model Peer Review Process and Recommendations for Best Practices, TBS
Guide on Peer Review (Draft) and associated checklist, the TBS Directive on Automated
Decision Making (Directive), to guide the assessment process.
Findings
Throughout the assessment process SSC discovered instances where provided
documentation did not fully reflect the current deployment of the system, and relevant
information from discussions was absent. All discovered discrepancies were reported to
IRCC for investigation. IRCC has confirmed their intent to resolve all discrepancies.
The review demonstrates that the IRCC staff have developed their models consistent with
TBS objectives for transparency and risk mitigation.
Recommendations
Fairness
Discrimination risk from relabeling records: Any adjustments (relabeling) made to the
training data should be carefully evaluated to ensure they do not introduce bias against
specific groups of applicants. If the data on infractions and the reversal of decisions from
2
approved to refused is not representative of all groups, there is a risk that the model could
learn to discriminate based on those biases. IRCC recognizes there is always inherent risk
of introducing bias when relabeling training data. As future iterations of the model are
developed, IRCC commits to continue monitoring the model to ensure no specific groups
of applicants experience bias.
Discrimination risk from external measurements and/or adverse events: In future
training iterations, it is recommended to clearly distinguish records that were approved or
refused based on permanent or temporary adverse events or external such as a changing
health situation, political/governmental factors or other adverse events to avoid
introducing permanent classifications or other forms of discrimination. As the final
decision is still overseen by an officer, the overall risk remains low but should be clearly
reported. IRCC will continue its practice of gathering information about changing country
conditions to ensure that decisions made by models and humans incorporate recent
developments. The risk of bias will be mitigated by vetting any new rules and by performing
Gender-Based Analysis Plus of the triage results.
Overreliance
Officer trust in the triage process may bias decisions: There is a risk that officers may
become overly reliant on the triage system, potentially impacting their decision-making on
admissibility. Officers may not navigate the multiple tabs and fields to appreciate the full
assessment of the model. It is important to emphasize that officers must maintain a
critical approach when reviewing applications, ensuring they do not blindly trust the
automated outputs. Additionally, there is a need to closely monitor decisions related to
adverse events to ensure they are thoroughly reviewed. IRCC recognizes that automation
bias is an inherent risk in all automation models. To account for this, the VR Model’s QA
plan includes a “blind” QA component to mitigate the risk of automation bias on decision-
makers.
Model
Model Validation through Cross-Validation would increase performance: To enhance
the model's generalization capabilities, it is recommended to implement cross-validation
techniques during training. This approach will help verify the model's performance on
unseen data and ensure that it is robust against overfitting, thereby improving its reliability
in real-world applications.
The IRCC Advanced Analytics Solution Centre (A2SC) decided that for future models,
cross-validation steps will be used for model training in order to prove robustness of the
model. However, the current model building procedures include a testing step which uses
30% of unseen test data to ensure absence of overfitting.
3
Privacy
Privacy Data Management and Security will continue to be ongoing Risks: As reported
in the provided documentation and associated annexes, the data used for training the
models contains sensitive personal information and is managed under strict security
protocols. Despite being hosted in a protected cloud environment, interfacing with other
systems and transferring data could pose potential risks. Ongoing vigilance and robust
security measures are essential to mitigate risks associated with data inconsistencies or
inaccuracy. IRCC will maintain its vigilance with respect to the internal dissemination of
personal information.
Data
Exploiting free text fields may bring value: SSC recommends conducting periodic
reviews of text mining results in the free text fields to verify data accuracy and control. The
business has asked the A2SC to perform text mining analysis of these fields. A2SC is in the
process of completing this analysis and will continue to do so periodically.
General Recommendations
Additional information discovered throughout the peer review process should be
integrated into the original documentation.
4
